meta.title: Lake Mead Water Elevation End-of-2027
meta.question: What will Lake Mead water surface elevation (feet above sea level) be on December 31, 2027?
meta.resolution: USBR official daily reservoir elevation reading for Lake Mead for December 31, 2027, as published on the Bureau of Reclamation Lower Colorado Region daily reservoir data portal. Fallback (in order): December 30 reading if Dec 31 is missing; SNWA published Lake Mead intake elevation for the same date; Western Area Power Administration Hoover Dam daily operations report. Reading is the end-of-day elevation in feet above sea level.

tagConfig._docs: Tag configuration for this topic. topicType presets: conflict, science, election, tech. Override directionHints for custom tag-to-hypothesis mappings.

directionHints.DATA: Check elevation readings and snowpack measurements

H1.label: Recovery: above 1,075 ft (exits Tier 1 shortage zone)

H2.label: Stable decline: 1,050-1,075 ft (remains in Tier 1 range)

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Lake Mead at ~1,061 ft (35% capacity, 20 ft above record low). BOR 24-month study projects possible record low (~1,032 ft) by Nov 2027. Very low 2025-26 snowpack. H3 (0.45): favored by BOR most-probable projection landing in 1,025-1,050 range. H2 (0.32): current-level inertia + conservation measures could hold elevation above 1,050 — requires near-average snowpack. H1 (0.08): would require above-average snowpack for 2 consecutive years to recover 14+ ft — historically rare during megadrought (occurred once since 2000, in 2023 winter). H4 (0.15): BOR CRSS model 10th-percentile exceedance runs show ~12-18% probability of sub-1,025 by end of 2027 depending on hydrology assumptions; 0.15 is consistent with the pessimistic-but-not-extreme tail.

tier1_critical.desc: USBR reading shows Lake Mead below 1,040.94 ft (2022 record low).
USBR reading shows Lake Mead below 1,025 ft (Tier 3 shortage threshold).
USBR reading shows Lake Mead above 1,075 ft (Tier 1 exit threshold).

tier2_strong.desc: NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack falls below 50% of median at April 1 measurement.
NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack exceeds 100% of median at April 1 measurement.
BOR 24-month study projects Lake Mead below 1,040 ft at any point within study window.
Lake Powell elevation drops below 3,500 ft (minimum power pool), reducing downstream releases to Mead.
NRCS SNOTEL Upper Colorado Basin snowpack exceeds 120% of median at any monthly measurement during Nov-Feb accumulation season.
BOR 24-month study projects Lake Mead below 1,025 ft at any point within study window.

tier3_suggestive.desc: Lake Mead drops more than 6 ft in any single calendar month.
SNWA or NPS publishes a public advisory, board resolution, or regulatory filing citing Lake Mead water temperature as a factor affecting water treatment operations or Hoover Dam turbine cooling.

NPS issues harmful algal bloom advisories for 3 or more named Lake Mead National Recreation Area locations (e.g., Boulder Beach, Callville Bay, Government Wash, Las Vegas Bay, Echo Bay) in the same NPS advisory or within the same 7-day window.
Any Lower Basin state enacts new mandatory water conservation measures beyond existing requirements.

anti_indicators.desc: Lake Mead end-of-month USBR elevation reading is lower than the start-of-month reading for two consecutive months during snowmelt season (Apr-Jul). Measured as: last daily reading of month N minus first daily reading of month N.
BOR 24-month study projects elevation below 1,050 ft by end of 2027.
Lake Mead elevation rises more than 5 ft in a single calendar month, as measured by USBR daily readings (month-start vs month-end).
Combined Colorado River system reservoir storage (Powell + Mead + Flaming Gorge + Navajo + Blue Mesa) rises above 40% of total capacity as reported in the BOR monthly operations summary.

actorModel.description: Institutional actors managing allocation within a physically driven system. Climate/snowpack is the primary driver (see physicalDrivers); actors manage the policy response but cannot override hydrology.

bor.name: Bureau of Reclamation
bor.role: Operates Glen Canyon and Hoover dams, sets release schedules, publishes 24-month studies, declares shortage tiers
bor.decisionStyle: Institutional/technocratic — follows hydrological models and legal framework (Law of the River)
biases: Model-dependent: projections anchor on recent hydrology
Institutional inertia: slow to deviate from established protocols
filters: Bound by Law of the River, DCP, 2007 Interim Guidelines
Must balance power generation vs water delivery
overrides: Emergency conditions (e.g., dam safety) override normal operations

snwa.name: Southern Nevada Water Authority
snwa.role: Las Vegas metro water supply; operates low-level pumping station (Intake 3)
snwa.decisionStyle: Risk-aware, engineering-first — invested in deep infrastructure
biases: May downplay severity given infrastructure investments
filters: Dependent on Lake Mead elevation for intake viability
overrides: Intake 3 can pump to dead pool — reduces urgency relative to other users

cap.name: Central Arizona Project
cap.role: Largest AZ water delivery system; junior priority rights (first cut in shortage)
cap.decisionStyle: Politically exposed, vocal advocate for maintaining allocations
biases: Lobbies to delay or soften shortage declarations
filters: Junior priority = absorbs cuts first under DCP
overrides: Severe shortage forces fallback to groundwater and alternative supplies

iid.name: Imperial Irrigation District
iid.role: Largest single Colorado River rights holder (3.1M AF/yr), senior priority
iid.decisionStyle: Protects senior rights aggressively; resists voluntary conservation
biases: Status quo bias: senior rights mean they are last cut
filters: Senior priority rights insulate from early shortage tiers
Agricultural economy dependent on full allocation
overrides: Federal intervention could override priority system

ucrc.name: Upper Colorado River Commission (CO, WY, UT, NM)
ucrc.role: Manages Upper Basin compact compliance; controls consumptive use that determines how much water reaches Powell and then Mead
ucrc.decisionStyle: Compact-driven, defensive — must deliver 7.5 MAF/yr to Lower Basin under 1922 Compact
biases: Climate is shrinking available supply faster than consumption reductions
filters: State water law and prior appropriation
overrides: Federal curtailment if compact deliveries are missed

mexico_ibwc.name: Mexico (CONAGUA) / International Boundary and Water Commission
mexico_ibwc.role: Mexico holds 1944 Treaty rights to 1.5 MAF/yr; IBWC manages binational water accounting; shortage sharing under Minute 323
mexico_ibwc.decisionStyle: Treaty-bound, diplomatic — proportional cuts under binational agreements
biases: Limited leverage but international dimension adds complexity
filters: Treaty obligations are senior to domestic DCP
Diplomatic channel separate from domestic politics
overrides: Severe crisis could prompt treaty renegotiation

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — only USBR data and official declarations move posteriors
TAG EVERYTHING — separate policy statements from hydrological data
SNOWPACK IS LEADING — April 1 snowpack is the single best predictor of summer inflow
PHYSICAL OVER POLITICAL — conservation measures slow decline but cannot reverse absent inflow

climate_hydrology.name: Climate/Hydrology
climate_hydrology.description: Snowpack, precipitation, evaporation — the physical driver of inflow
trends: Multi-decadal aridification trend (Colorado flow declining ~10% per decade)
exceptions: Exceptional wet years can temporarily reverse trends (e.g., 2023)

evidenceLog.text: Lake Mead dropped approximately 6 ft since March 1, 2026, now sitting 20 ft above the all-time low. Currently at approximately 35% capacity. SNWA outreach manager Bronson Mack stated the lake could drop another 16 ft by 2027. Federal projections released in March 2026 indicate Lake Mead could reach record-low water levels (~1,032 ft) by November 2027. Previous record low was approximately 1,040.94 ft, set in 2022. Very low snowpack cited as primary driver. Rising water temperatures reported as affecting water treatment operations. NPS issued harmful algal bloom advisory for Government Wash on March 13.
evidenceLog.source: The Independent (citing FOX 5 Vegas, Nevada Current, NPS)
evidenceLog.posteriorImpact: NONE. Multiple indicators are directionally relevant but none have fired: t1_below_record requires USBR reading below 1,040.94 ft (current ~1,041 ft, not yet breached). t2_snowpack_below_50 requires April 1 SNOTEL measurement (described as very low but no numeric). t3_water_temp_alert requires formal SNWA advisory (concerns cited but no formal advisory). t3_algal_bloom_multi requires 3+ named locations (only Government Wash). H3-directional given BOR projection of ~1,032 ft, but no threshold crossed.
evidenceLog.note: Seed evidence. Source: The Independent (0.50 initial trust), citing FOX 5 Vegas local reporting and Nevada Current.

upper_colorado_snowpack.label: Upper Colorado Basin Snowpack (% of median)

searchQueries: Bureau of Reclamation 24-month study Colorado River {date}

dependencies._docs: Cross-topic dependency declarations. Upstream topics whose posteriors this topic's assumptions depend on. Checked mechanically on save — stale assumptions surface as governance issues.

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots are auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when topic resolves, then compute_calibration_report() for Brier analysis.
